cves.description:
evernote-mcp-server openBrowser Command Injection Privilege Escalation Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows local attackers to escalate privileges on affected installations of evernote-mcp-server. An attacker must first obtain the ability to execute low-privileged code on the target system in order to exploit this vulnerability.

The specific flaw exists within the openBrowser function. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of a user-supplied string before using it to execute
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-authored commit e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146 created the vulnerable `openBrowser()` implementation with `child_process.exec()` and shell interpolation of the URL, and in the same feature added the OAuth flow that builds `authUrl` from the request token and passes it into that function. Later AI-authored commits only changed surrounding logging/config behavior and did not introduce the command injection primitive.
cves.root_cause: The openBrowser function failed to properly validate or sanitize user-supplied input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary commands into the system shell execution.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Passing unvalidated user input directly into a system command execution sink (e.g., child_process.exec or similar) within the openBrowser function.

bug_commits.author: brentmid
bug_commits.message: Add comprehensive OAuth 1.0a authentication and testing suite

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the entire initial implementation of `auth.js`, including the use of `child_process.exec` to open the system browser. This specific implementation used template literals to concatenate a user-controlled URL directly into a shell command, which is the root cause of the command injection vulnerability fixed in the subsequent commit.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The openBrowser function failed to properly validate or sanitize user-supplied input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary commands into the system shell execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Passing unvalidated user input directly into a system command execution sink (e.g., child_process.exec or similar) within the openBrowser function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: 1. Blamed commit e08547 introduces auth.js and the child_process.exec dependency. 2. It implements the openBrowser function using shell execution: exec(`${command} "${url}"`). 3. This pattern allows an attacker to break out of the URL string and execute arbitrary system commands via the shell.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit created `auth.js` and introduced the exact code the fix removes: `const { exec } = require('child_process');` and `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, ...)` inside `openBrowser()`. It also introduced `redirectToAuthorization()` with `const authUrl = `${EVERNOTE_CONFIG.authorizeUrl}?oauth_token=${requestToken}`;` followed by `openBrowser(authUrl)`, so the same commit both authored the unsafe shell execution and wired attacker-influenced OAuth data into it. Pickaxe searches for the vulnerable strings show their first appearance in this commit, matching the screening hypothesis.

cves.ai_contribution: Yes. AI-authored commit e08547bcdb42aaa86190c6e2dfc64159fcd3a146 created the vulnerable `openBrowser()` implementation with `child_process.exec()` and shell interpolation of the URL, and in the same feature added the OAuth flow that builds `authUrl` from the request token and passes it into that function. Later AI-authored commits only changed surrounding logging/config behavior and did not introduce the command injection primitive.

cves.description: A vulnerability has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function sort_cmp of the file src/array.c. Such manipulation leads to use after free. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The name of the patch is eb398971bfb43c38db3e04528b68ac9a7ce509bc. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: AI-assisted code in cf8faed585e14ab57cc390173d1b571aee438390 rewrote sort_cmp and left only a pointer-equality modification check (`RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`), removing the earlier size-based guard and directly writing the lines later fixed by eb398971. The original stale-pointer design came from an earlier human commit, but the AI-assisted reimplementation/regression materially contributed to the vulnerable state that shipped.
cves.root_cause: The Array#sort! function in mruby suffered from a use-after-realloc vulnerability within sort_cmp, where the array buffer could be reallocated during the sorting process, causing existing pointers to become invalid.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing array elements or pointers that may have been invalidated by a realloc call triggered within the sort comparison function.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: array: implement hybrid sorting with algorithm selection

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `sort_cmp` function signature and logic that relied on the pointer `p` to detect array modifications. By storing the pointer `p` as the base address of the array at the start of the sort and only checking `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p` later, it failed to account for scenarios where `realloc` changes the buffer size without changing the base pointer, or where the length of the array changes in a way that invalidates the previously captured pointer `p` before the check occurs.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The Array#sort! function in mruby suffered from a use-after-realloc vulnerability within sort_cmp, where the array buffer could be reallocated during the sorting process, causing existing pointers to become invalid.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing array elements or pointers that may have been invalidated by a realloc call triggered within the sort comparison function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced a comparison mechanism in `sort_cmp` that captures a pointer `p` (RARRAY_PTR(ary)) to detect array reallocations. However, this check was insufficient to detect all forms of reallocations or modifications (specifically those changing length or buffer content without necessarily changing the head pointer), allowing the heapify or insertion sort logic to continue using stale pointers into a reallocated buffer, leading to use-after-free.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This AI-assisted commit rewrote `sort_cmp` to take element values instead of indices, added `insertion_sort`, and replaced the earlier post-callback guard with a pointer-only check: `if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p)`. The fix commit eb398971 changes exactly this logic by snapshotting both pointer and length and checking `RARRAY_LEN(ary) != n`, so cf8faed directly authored the insufficient check that the patch corrects. The screening hypothesis is directionally correct: this commit did not just move code; it removed the prior length/index-based safeguard and left the comparison path vulnerable to in-place shrink/realloc that preserves the same pointer.

cves.ai_contribution: AI-assisted code in cf8faed585e14ab57cc390173d1b571aee438390 rewrote sort_cmp and left only a pointer-equality modification check (`RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p`), removing the earlier size-based guard and directly writing the lines later fixed by eb398971. The original stale-pointer design came from an earlier human commit, but the AI-assisted reimplementation/regression materially contributed to the vulnerable state that shipped.

cves.description: vet MCP Server SSE Transport DNS Rebinding Vulnerability
cves.how_introduced: Copilot-authored commit cd7caffb4aa568d07edc0d21bbd7dabd38ef7e2c did not originate the unguarded SSE transport, but it added a new `/sse` HEAD handler that explicitly returned `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` and continued serving the transport through an unguarded HTTP handler. bug_commits.message: Add HTTP HEAD request support to SSE MCP server

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced `sseHandlerWithHeadSupport`, which explicitly set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` and failed to implement any origin or Host header validation for SSE requests. This established the insecure handling pattern that allowed the DNS rebinding vulnerability, which was subsequently mitigated in the fix commit by introducing `originGuard` and `hostGuard` wrappers.

screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of Host header verification or origin checking in SSE transport connections, allowing unauthorized requests to bypass same-origin protections.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit added an HTTP handler that accepted SSE requests without validating the origin or Host header, and specifically included a permissive wildcard CORS policy (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *), thereby creating the vulnerable surface area.

It also created the custom http.Server and sseHandlerWithHeadSupport handler chain that the fix (0ae3560) directly patches by replacing the wildcard CORS with origin-reflective headers and adding hostGuard/originGuard middleware. While the pre-BIC code using s.Start() also lacked host validation, the BIC explicitly added the permissive wildcard CORS policy and created the exact handler infrastructure that needed hardening. cves.description: A flaw has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This affects the function mrb_vm_exec of the file src/vm.c of the component JMPNOT-to-JMPIF Optimization. Executing a manipulation can lead to use after free. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been published and may be used. This patch is called e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The core bug fixed by e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415 was introduced by AI-coauthored commit 2b72d8a7c153e2afb22245ad9e40e0c7d5b1aa70, which added a JMPNOT-to-JMPIF rewrite that blindly wrote `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF` without verifying the opcode at that location was actually `OP_JMPNOT`. Earlier/later AI-coauthored commits 1de6340f1bc81564274890660f66444e48d660b0 and bbd5dd690b9f47977a092e5e9b347f0960e6d6c0 supplied additional `OP_JMP` producers in the same fail-chain, but the CVE-fixed vulnerability itself comes from the unsafe optimizer added in 2b72d8a7.
cves.root_cause: The JMPNOT-to-JMPIF bytecode optimization creates bytecode corruption leading to a use-after-free condition in the mruby virtual machine during execution.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect bytecode generation logic during JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization that produces invalid jump targets or corrupts stack state

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize JMPNOT+JMP to JMPIF in pattern matching

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization logic which lacked validation to ensure the instruction being replaced was actually an OP_JMPNOT. The fix subsequently added this missing check (`s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT`), confirming that the original implementation allowed incorrect instructions to be overwritten, leading to the bytecode corruption.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The JMPNOT-to-JMPIF bytecode optimization creates bytecode corruption leading to a use-after-free condition in the mruby virtual machine during execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect bytecode generation logic during JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization that produces invalid jump targets or corrupts stack state
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented an optimization that assumes the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is always `OP_JMPNOT`. Without this check, other instruction types (like JMP from undefined variables) could be incorrectly overwritten with `OP_JMPIF`, corrupting the bytecode execution and triggering the use-after-free.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the exact optimization block that the fix modifies, including the vulnerable write `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF;` guarded only by chain/adjacency checks. The fix e50f15c1 adds the missing opcode validation `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT` to this same block, confirming that 2b72d8a7 authored the faulty assumption. Before 2b72d8a7, the corresponding code simply emitted `match_pos = genjmp(...);` and then `dispatch_linked(s, fail_pos);`, so no prior instruction bytes were rewritten and this corruption path did not exist.

cves.description: eBay API MCP Server Affected by Environment Variable Injection
cves.how_introduced: AI-authored code directly contributed: commit 4c9c826c6fc8b64a20e948cb46fefdda42d5244d (author: Claude) introduced the vulnerable updateEnvFile() helper and wired it into token refresh/persistence paths, using raw string interpolation to rewrite .env entries. A later human commit co-authored with Claude (8c1989e36ad2950fcf4c8f9f95027ebf487e0f61) rewrote the same sink to add quotes but still failed to escape attacker-controlled values, so AI remained part of the causal chain.
cves.root_cause: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.

bug_commits.author: Claude
bug_commits.message: feat: Auto-update .env file with refreshed tokens

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `updateEnvFile` function, which uses simple string replacement and concatenation to write user-provided tokens (like access and refresh tokens) directly into the `.env` file without proper quoting or escaping. This implementation pattern allows for environment variable injection because an attacker-supplied token containing newlines or extra assignments could modify or append arbitrary entries to the .env file.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced the `updateEnvFile` utility using a flawed regex-based string manipulation approach (`const newLine = ${key}=${value};`), which directly concatenates untrusted input into the environment file configuration, directly enabling the reported injection vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the first introduction of the vulnerable .env-writing mechanism. Its diff adds updateEnvFile(), which reads the .env file as raw text, constructs lines with direct interpolation (`const newLine = `${key}=${value}``), and rewrites the file via regex/string replacement; it also adds the updateEnvFile() call sites in setUserTokens(), getOrRefreshAppAccessToken(), and refreshUserToken(). Pickaxe searches for `function updateEnvFile(`, `envContent = envContent.replace(regex, newLine);`, `writeFileSync(envPath, envContent, 'utf-8');`, and `updateEnvFile({` all trace this pattern back to 4c9c826c, so the screening hypothesis is correct.

This permitted an attacker to inject characters (like `"`) into the OAuth process, which the subsequent vulnerable `updateEnvFile` function then wrote into the .env file, leading to arbitrary configuration injection.

That addressed `#` truncation but still inserted untrusted token data directly into .env syntax without escaping embedded quotes or newlines, which is exactly the line later removed by the fix in favor of dotenv.parse/stringify. cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-assisted commits `9f66c42f06f3b87107ce327bede6416a582f0e60` and `6bcd260679f3838743cad07da62628487267e914` directly introduced the flawed pre-shard recovery logic: one enabled shard doubling/random routing without a working path to materialize non-zero shard buckets, and the other added broken proactive sharding plus shard propagation that produced unusable shard items. Those defects kept traffic concentrated on a single usable DynamoDB partition and allowed hot-entity denial of service.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `_is_wcu_exhausted` logic and the initial handling of speculative failures related to shard limits, which the vulnerability analysis identifies as flawed. Specifically, this commit added the rudimentary check that failed to account for correct capacity aggregation and proper handling of DynamoDB throttling states, which the fix commit subsequently replaces with more granular `SpeculativeFailureReason` checks.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented `_is_wcu_exhausted` using a simplified logic check (comparing tokens_milli < 1000) that failed to correctly interpret DynamoDB partition throttling states. This logic error meant that when the system encountered high contention or throttling, it incorrectly triggered shard doubling or retries without proper deduplication or capacity validation, ultimately leading to the reported DoS condition.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Blame points at `_is_wcu_exhausted()`, but that threshold is not the real bug: the fix keeps the same `<1000` WCU test and only moves the classification into `SpeculativeFailureReason`. The real problem introduced here is that this commit added random shard selection, retry, and `bump_shard_count()` while the slow path still fetched and created only shard 0 (`_fetch_buckets()` calls `batch_get_buckets()`/`get_buckets()` with no shard id, and `lease.py` calls `build_composite_create()` without passing `shard_id`). That changed the calling context of shard-0-only code and left doubled shards effectively unusable, so hot-entity traffic remained concentrated on one partition.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(aggregator): filter wcu, proactive sharding, shard propagation

AI-assisted commit 3b0c80ce245b293fae817ec800c5e3a9c0ec7ee8 (Claude/claude_code) directly introduced the vulnerable `toolsBySender` design and matcher: it flattened sender IDs, phone numbers, usernames, and display names into one normalized key space, then matched all of those candidate fields against the same map. That is exactly the behavior the fix replaces with typed `id:/e164:/username:/name:` buckets and an ID-only legacy fallback.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to properly distinguish between different sender identities in 'toolsBySender' configurations, leading to potential identity-collision where a user could inadvertently or maliciously assume permissions assigned to a different user with a matching key.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Insufficiently specific sender-key matching logic in group policy validation that allowed cross-identity access

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `resolveToolsBySender` function, which utilized a single, non-typed `normalizeSenderKey` function to process all types of sender identifiers (ID, username, e164, name) into a unified key space. This flawed logic allowed different sender types to collide in the `normalized` Map, enabling a user with one type of identity to potentially match a policy intended for a completely different identity type (e.g., a username colliding with a numeric sender ID).
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to properly distinguish between different sender identities in 'toolsBySender' configurations, leading to potential identity-collision where a user could inadvertently or maliciously assume permissions assigned to a different user with a matching key.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Insufficiently specific sender-key matching logic in group policy validation that allowed cross-identity access
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit implemented `resolveToolsBySender` using a simplified normalization strategy that failed to distinguish between identifier types, effectively creating an ambiguous key space where cross-identity collisions were possible.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This same commit introduced `GroupToolPolicyBySenderConfig` as an untyped `Record<string, GroupToolPolicyConfig>` and added schema/docs describing keys as 'sender IDs/usernames/emails/phone numbers depending on the channel.' The fix later documents explicit prefixes (`id:`, `e164:`, `username:`, `name:`) and treats legacy unprefixed keys as deprecated ID-only, reversing that original design. While the runtime exploit sits in `group-policy.ts`, this file's addition enabled the vulnerable feature without any required type distinction, so the screening hypothesis for `src/config/types.tools.ts` is also correct.

cves.description: Heap-buffer-overflow in tinyobj::tryParseDouble
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-authored commit f27721f3c9cd20af4d4eee789431b92d0e0c4477 rewrote parsing to use exact-length StreamReader buffers and passed raw buffer slices (`start`, `start + len`) into the legacy `tryParseDouble`, making its pre-existing out-of-bounds read reachable on malformed EOF-terminated numbers. The parser bug itself predates AI, but AI code was part of the causal chain that turned it into this exploitable heap-buffer-overflow.
cves.root_cause: The parser was susceptible to a heap-buffer-overflow during the conversion of ASCII strings to floating-point numbers due to unsafe parsing logic.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: unsafe floating point parsing implementation in tinyobj::tryParseDouble, likely lacking bounds checking or proper termination handling.

bug_commits.author: copilot-swe-agent[bot]
bug_commits.message: Add max_iter guards to parsing loops and mmap support for .obj/.mtl loading

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced a significant rework of the parser implementation in tiny_obj_loader.h, including the introduction of new parsing logic that often lacks the rigorous bounds checking required for safe floating-point conversion, which is the root cause of the heap-buffer-overflow.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The parser was susceptible to a heap-buffer-overflow during the conversion of ASCII strings to floating-point numbers due to unsafe parsing logic.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: unsafe floating point parsing implementation in tinyobj::tryParseDouble, likely lacking bounds checking or proper termination handling.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit 5df014602503 refactored the parsing infrastructure in tiny_obj_loader.h and introduced a vulnerable implementation of tryParseDouble that did not correctly validate input lengths or boundaries before processing numeric strings, leading to the heap-buffer-overflow during float conversion.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The screening hypothesis is only half-right: this commit did not write the vulnerable `tryParseDouble` implementation itself—blame on the fix parent shows almost all of that function still comes from 03a2f3a, with f277 only touching an unrelated exponent-overflow guard line. However, f277 introduced `StreamReader`, the new `sr_parseReal` helpers, and calls such as `tryParseDouble(start, start + len, &val)` over exact-length buffers, while public `LoadObj`/`LoadMtl` paths were switched from `linebuf.c_str()` to `StreamReader`. That calling-context change removed the old implicit NUL terminator, so malformed final tokens like `1e` at EOF can make `tryParseDouble` dereference past `s_end`, making this commit a real introducer of the exploitable CVE.

cves.description: Claude Code permission deny bypass through symlink
cves.how_introduced: AI-authored code in the hookify plugin directly introduced one of the vulnerable paths: commit 59372c0921b0170e81f9c63777962d02347411d5 created RuleEngine file-path checks that compared raw user-supplied path strings instead of canonicalized real paths, enabling symlink-based deny bypasses. A separate human-authored plugin (security-guidance) introduced the same class of flaw, but AI code was still part of the causal chain for the CVE because it wrote vulnerable permission-checking logic that the fix later reversed.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to resolve symlinks before evaluating deny rules, allowing an attacker to bypass access controls by pointing a path to a restricted location.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Evaluating file system path permissions without canonicalizing paths or resolving symbolic links, leading to path traversal bypasses.

bug_commits.author: Daisy S. Hollman
bug_commits.message: feat: Add hookify plugin for custom hook rules via markdown

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `RuleEngine` class and its initial implementation for evaluating rules. By failing to include symlink resolution when parsing and checking `file_path` inputs, it established the vulnerable pattern that allowed access control bypasses from the very beginning of the component's existence.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to resolve symlinks before evaluating deny rules, allowing an attacker to bypass access controls by pointing a path to a restricted location.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Evaluating file system path permissions without canonicalizing paths or resolving symbolic links, leading to path traversal bypasses.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit created the `RuleEngine` logic, which accepted file paths for permission checks without canonicalization. Since it lacked any mechanism to resolve symlinks, all subsequent access control checks based on path strings were inherently vulnerable to symlink-based path traversal attacks.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: [conflict-resolver] The blamed commit 59372c0 created the entire rule_engine.py from scratch, including the _extract_field method that returns file_path values from tool_input without any symlink resolution (e.g., `return tool_input.get('file_path', '')` and the generic `if field in tool_input: value = tool_input[field]; return value`). The fix commit ff0fdc0 modifies exactly these code paths in rule_engine.py to add _resolve_symlink_path() calls using os.path.realpath(). The fix also adds Read to the file event tools in pretooluse.py, which was also created by the blamed commit without Read. The deep verifier correctly identified that the core vulnerability is the unresolved symlink comparison in rule_engine.py, but incorrectly concluded this means the BIC is UNLIKELY — in fact, the BIC created that exact vulnerable code. Both the screening and deep verifier agree on the root cause (rule_engine.py lacking symlink resolution); they only disagree on whether this implicates the BIC, which it clearly does since 59372c0 wrote the vulnerable code from scratch.

cves.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of role-based or permission-based checks when rendering sensitive data (keys/credentials) in UI components and processing requests

By explicitly using the `|safe` filter without additional sanitization filters, it allowed user-controlled input to be rendered directly into the JavaScript context, creating the Stored XSS vulnerability.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application fails to properly sanitize the 'custom_hotkeys' user profile field, allowing attackers to inject malicious scripts that execute when viewed, enabling account takeover.

In that template, they rendered the input using the `|safe` Jinja filter, which explicitly tells the template engine not to escape the output, directly resulting in the XSS vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the first one to add the exact XSS sink that the fix changes: `var __customHotkeys = {{ user.custom_hotkeys|json_dumps_ensure_ascii|safe }};` in `label_studio/templates/base.html`, and pickaxe shows that string originates here. The same commit also added `custom_hotkeys` to `BaseUserSerializer`, and `BaseUserSerializerUpdate` inherits those fields, while `UserAPI` uses `UserSerializerUpdate` for PATCH requests; because `UserAPI.get_queryset()` targets any user in the active organization and PATCH requires only `organizations_view`, this commit made `custom_hotkeys` writable through the generic user-by-id endpoint. Since all permissions are globally registered to `rules.is_authenticated`, this feature commit both created the stored-XSS sink and enabled the vulnerable field on the existing IDOR path.

cves.description: Babylon Incorrect FP inactive accounting in costaking creates “phantom stake” that earns rewards after BTC unbond
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The first commit that introduced the buggy costaking unbonding logic is 2bd9585607bd7b1c0905d21b92bcdb6d4c03bf36, an AI-attributed backport commit with a Copilot co-author trailer, and that commit directly added the flawed `if !isFpActiveInPrevSet || !isFpActiveInCurrSet` guard that leaves phantom stake behind when a finality provider becomes inactive during BTC unbonding.
cves.root_cause: The costaking mechanism incorrectly accounted for inactive finality provider (FP) stakes, allowing 'phantom stakes' to continue earning rewards after a BTC unbonding process had occurred.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Improper state management or hook logic in the costaking module that failed to correctly invalidate or update stake accounting when a finality provider became inactive or an unbonding event triggered.

bug_commits.author: RafilxTenfen
bug_commits.message: feat: costaking (#1707)

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `AfterBtcDelegationUnbonded` function with the flawed logic that required both `isFpActiveInPrevSet` and `isFpActiveInCurrSet` to be true before subtracting satoshis. This incorrect condition caused the system to fail to unbond stake correctly in scenarios where an FP transition occurred, directly creating the 'phantom stake' vulnerability described in the CVE.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The costaking mechanism incorrectly accounted for inactive finality provider (FP) stakes, allowing 'phantom stakes' to continue earning rewards after a BTC unbonding process had occurred.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Improper state management or hook logic in the costaking module that failed to correctly invalidate or update stake accounting when a finality provider became inactive or an unbonding event triggered.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented the hook `AfterBtcDelegationUnbonded` with an `if !isFpActiveInPrevSet || !isFpActiveInCurrSet` check, which prevented proper stake accounting updates when a finality provider transitioned to an inactive state during the unbonding process, thereby leaving phantom stakes in the system.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix changes `x/costaking/keeper/hooks_finality.go` so `AfterBtcDelegationUnbonded` returns early only when the FP was not active in the previous set; before the fix it returned early whenever the FP was inactive in the current set too. Pickaxe shows 2bd95856 is the first commit to add both `AfterBtcDelegationUnbonded` and the exact vulnerable conditional `if !isFpActiveInPrevSet || !isFpActiveInCurrSet`, and blame on the vulnerable lines points to this same commit. The same commit also had the now-reverted rationale about avoiding 'double subtraction', but `x/finality/keeper/power_dist_change.go` already processed BTC-delegation hooks before FP-removal hooks and `x/incentive/keeper/reward_tracker_store.go` already made `IterateBTCDelegationSatsUpdated` account for pending events, so the FP-removal hook would subtract only the updated remaining active sats. As a result, when an unbond causes an FP to leave the active set, the unbonded sats are never subtracted from the costaker tracker, creating the phantom stake fixed later.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Generic webhook channel trusts caller-supplied identity fields; allowlist is checked against untrusted payload data
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-assisted code in commit 2c9deefbf744089c3041885717b92c6f2fc0bf8c (co-authored by Claude / flagged as claude_code) introduced the generic webhook feature with payload-driven identity: it accepted caller-supplied `sender` and `chat_id`, checked the allowlist against `payload.sender`, and published those untrusted values as the inbound identity. The fix reverses that design by adding server-controlled `sender_id`/`chat_id`, defaulting `trust_payload_identity` to false, and only using payload identity as an explicit legacy opt-in.
cves.root_cause: The webhook channel trusted user-supplied identity fields without proper validation, allowing attackers to spoof identities by passing untrusted payload data against the allowlist.
cves.vuln_type: Improper Authentication / Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Relying on caller-supplied identity fields in webhook payloads without verifying their authenticity or validating them against a trusted, server-side configuration.

cves.description: OpenClaw versions prior to 2026.2.13 contain a denial of service vulnerability in webhook handlers that buffer request bodies without strict byte or time limits. Remote unauthenticated attackers can send oversized JSON payloads or slow uploads to webhook endpoints causing memory pressure and availability degradation.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The AI-assisted commit 5c2cb6c591e4b63c2df0549ad2202403256e2a96 (co-authored by Claude and flagged as claude_code) introduced Feishu webhook mode and registered `Lark.adaptDefault(path, eventDispatcher, { autoChallenge: true })` directly on an HTTP server without any body-size or body-time limits; the fix wraps that exact handler in `installRequestBodyLimitGuard`. Earlier AI-assisted Feishu commits only implemented websocket behavior or explicitly left webhook mode unimplemented, so they did not make the vulnerable request-body path reachable.

cves.description: A weakness has been identified in mruby 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function ary_fill_exec of the file mrbgems/mruby-array-ext/src/array.c. Executing a manipulation of the argument start/length can lead to out-of-bounds write. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been made available to the public and could be used for attacks. This patch is called 93619f06dd378db6766666b30c08978311c7ec94. It is best practice to apply a patch to resolve this issue.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. AI-coauthored commit 9e8cda73f69493012c8784667b03016c2072ed2b introduced the vulnerable `ary_fill_exec` helper and exposed it as `Array#__fill_exec` without rejecting negative `start`/`length`, allowing callers to bypass the normalizing parser and reach the out-of-bounds write fixed by 93619f06dd378db6766666b30c08978311c7ec94.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-array-ext: implement Array#fill in C

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit first added `ary_fill_exec` and registered it as the Ruby instance method `__fill_exec`. The added implementation takes raw `start`/`length` with `mrb_get_args(mrb, "iio", &start, &length, &obj);`, performs no negative-value validation, then does `mrb_value *ptr = ARY_PTR(ary) + start;` and writes through that pointer, exactly the unsafe pattern fixed later by adding `start < 0` / `length < 0` checks. Pickaxe searches for both the `mrb_get_args(..."iio"...)` line and the `__fill_exec` method definition trace their introduction to this commit, and blame on the vulnerable executable lines at the fix parent points to this commit as well.

cves.description:
Protection mechanism failure in wolfCrypt post-quantum implementations (ML-KEM and ML-DSA) in wolfSSL on ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers allows a physical attacker to compromise key material and/or cryptographic outcomes via induced transient faults that corrupt or redirect seed/pointer values during Keccak-based expansion.




This issue affects wolfSSL (wolfCrypt): commit hash d86575c766e6e67ef93545fa69c04d6eb49400c6.
cves.how_introduced: Devin AI directly contributed by creating the new ML-KEM implementation file `wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem.c` with the same shared-buffer `rho`/`sigma` seed-pointer design that the fix later hardens with separate storage and integrity checks. Even though the pattern existed earlier in human-authored Kyber code, this AI-authored reimplementation was a new vulnerable code path and part of the causal chain for the CVE.

bug_commits.author: Devin AI
bug_commits.message: Update Kyber APIs to ML-KEM APIs

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This AI-authored commit created `wolfcrypt/src/wc_mlkem.c` and introduced `wc_MlKemKey_MakeKeyWithRandom()` with `byte buf[2 * KYBER_SYM_SZ + 1]; byte* rho = buf; byte* sigma = buf + KYBER_SYM_SZ;`, then passed `sigma` into noise/keygen routines. The fix reverses that design by moving `sigma` to separate storage and verifying the copied seed, which is exactly the fault-hardening missing here. Although later rename work changed some identifiers, this commit wrote the vulnerable ML-KEM implementation file, so under the reimplementation rule it is a true BIC.

cves.description:
HTTP::Session2 versions before 1.12 for Perl for Perl may generate weak session ids using the rand() function.

The HTTP::Session2 session id generator returns a SHA-1 hash seeded with the built-in rand function, the epoch time, and the PID. The PID will come from a small set of numbers, and the epoch time may be guessed, if it is not leaked from the HTTP Date header. The built-in rand() function is unsuitable for cryptographic usage.

cves.description: A path traversal vulnerability exists in Zen MCP Server before 9.8.2 that allows authenticated attackers to read arbitrary files on the system. The vulnerability is caused by flawed logic in the is_dangerous_path() validation function that uses exact string matching against a blacklist of system directories. Attackers can bypass these restrictions by accessing subdirectories of blacklisted paths.
cves.how_introduced: Yes. The confirmed introducer is 4151c3c3a59f519577b18ed9a885d0d32c1b566f, an explicitly Claude Code-assisted commit, which replaced the prior workspace allowlist sandbox with a new blacklist-based path validator and implemented the flawed exact-match `is_dangerous_path()` logic that allowed `/etc/passwd`-style subdirectory bypasses.

bug_commits.message: Migration from Docker to Standalone Python Server (#73)

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Pickaxe shows both `def is_dangerous_path(path: Path) -> bool:` and the vulnerable line `return str(resolved) in DANGEROUS_PATHS or resolved.parent == resolved` were first added in this commit. Its diff also changed `resolve_and_validate_path()` from a safe workspace sandbox (`resolved_path.relative_to(SECURITY_ROOT)`) to the new blacklist check `if is_dangerous_path(resolved_path):`, making arbitrary absolute-path access depend on the flawed exact-match logic. Blame on the vulnerable lines in both `utils/security_config.py` and `utils/file_utils.py` points to this commit, so it directly wrote the vulnerable function and the calling context that made it exploitable.

The blamed commit (f972f8617445) introduces the EmailChannel module configuration and logic, which deals with network protocols (IMAP/SMTP), not local file path handling or boundary validation. The fix commit (bf004a20d368) merely adds documentation warnings about sender authentication, which is unrelated to the file system security issues described in the CVE.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application fails to properly validate file paths against boundaries, allowing symlink or hardlink attacks (TOCTOU) to access unauthorized files.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the first introduction of the vulnerable design. It added `src/channels/email_channel.rs`, the `EmailConfig.allowed_senders` surface in `src/config/types.rs`, and factory wiring for the email channel; the new code parses the inbound `From` header (`parsed.from()...address()`) and authorizes messages with `is_sender_allowed(&from)` using only that header value.
